title: TSCG: Deterministic Tool-Schema Compilation for Agentic LLM Deployments
problem: JSON tool schemas written for machine parsing cause tool-use failures when interpreted by LLMs, especially small (4B-14B) models at production catalog sizes.
unit_of_analysis: Tool-use accuracy per (model x catalog size); ~19000 function calls across 12 models.
intervention: Deterministically recompile JSON tool schemas into token-efficient structured text without model access, fine-tuning, or runtime search.
method: Eight composable, label-free compression operators with a formal >=51% compression bound; profiles models as operator-hungry/-sensitive/-robust.
data: TSCG-Agentic-Bench (~19000 calls), BFCL, and real MCP schemas.
main_claim: A deterministic, label-free schema compiler restores Phi-4 14B from 0% to 84.4% at 20 tools and yields 52-57% token savings while retaining/raising accuracy.
relation_to_ours: CLOSEST ANCHOR. Shares the deterministic, label-free transformation-of-tool-representation idea that underlies our normalization layer. BUT it does deterministic schema textualization/compression (explicitly inside our novelty boundary), does NOT define interface equivalence by environment state-transition equality, does NOT do metamorphic paired evaluation, does NOT study ranking reversal, and does NOT frame normalization as transferring to UNSEEN transforms. Our normal form is learned/semantic and transform-label-independent, not a fixed operator set.

title: Schema First Tool APIs for LLM Agents: A Controlled Study of Tool Misuse Recovery and Budgeted Performance
problem: Whether tool interface specification + validation feedback improves agent reliability under strict interaction budgets; interface design is understudied as an experimental variable.
unit_of_analysis: Repository tasks in a deterministic SE sandbox; 1 local model x 3 seeds x 3 interface conditions x 4 budgets.
intervention: Vary ONLY the tool interface specification (free-form NL docs vs strict JSON Schema vs JSON Schema + structured validation diagnostics) while holding environment, task semantics, and information content constant.
method: Controlled factorial study measuring interface misuse, semantic misuse, recovery, and budgeted success.
data: Deterministic SE/repository sandbox with logs and metrics.
main_claim: Schema conditions reduce interface misuse but not semantic misuse; success stays ~0, so interface formalization helps contract adherence but semantic action quality remains the bottleneck.
relation_to_ours: VERY HIGH on experimental design: explicitly holds tool semantics/information content constant and varies only interface representation, which is the core manipulation we claim. BUT it varies spec style (NL vs JSON vs JSON+diagnostics) not behavior-preserving transforms tied to a formal state-transition equivalence; no metamorphic PAIRED design, no model-ranking analysis (single model), and no normalization layer / unseen-transform transfer. We add equivalence-by-state-transition, paired metamorphic eval, ranking reversal across models, and an automatic normal form.

title: ReliabilityBench: Evaluating LLM Agent Reliability Under Production-Like Stress Conditions
problem: Agent benchmarks report only single-run success and ignore production reliability (consistency, robustness to perturbation, fault tolerance).
unit_of_analysis: 1280 episodes; 2 models (Gemini 2.0 Flash, GPT-4o) x 2 architectures (ReAct, Reflexion) x 4 domains.
intervention: Inject three stressors: repeated execution (pass^k), semantically-equivalent task perturbations (intensity eps), and tool/API faults (intensity lambda) including schema drift.
method: Chaos-engineering fault injection + 'Action Metamorphic Relations' whose equivalence criterion is END-STATE equivalence rather than text similarity.
main_claim: Perturbations and faults significantly degrade reliability (96.9%->88.1% at eps=0.2; rate limiting most damaging); end-state-based scoring is more faithful than text matching.
relation_to_ours: HIGH and most dangerous on terminology: it already names 'metamorphic relations' AND defines correctness by END-STATE equivalence -- two phrases close to our (1) state-transition equality and (2) metamorphic evaluation. BUT it perturbs the TASK/environment and injects faults (which CHANGE the state transition / inject schema drift as a failure), whereas we hold the state transition IDENTICAL and change only the model-visible interface representation. It does not study ranking reversal driven by representation, and has no normalization layer / unseen-transform transfer. We must explicitly differentiate 'metamorphic over interface representation with invariant state transition' from their 'metamorphic over tasks/faults'.

title: Learning to Rewrite Tool Descriptions for Reliable LLM-Agent Tool Use
authors: Ruocheng Guo; Kaiwen Dong; Xiang Gao; Kamalika Das
unit_of_analysis: Tool-use plateaus because human-written tool descriptions are ambiguous; per-tool optimization is expensive and does not generalize to new tools.
intervention: Tool interface descriptions across multi-tool scenarios (150+ tools).
method: Train a model to rewrite tool descriptions into agent-friendly form that transfers WITHOUT per-tool traces at test time.
data: Trace-Free+ curriculum learning that progressively moves from trace-rich to trace-free supervision, internalizing reusable description patterns.
main_claim: Synthesized large-scale high-quality tool-interface dataset; StableToolBench and other benchmarks.
relation_to_ours: A learned rewriter generalizes across domains/unseen tools without retraining and improves tool-use reliability.
collision_risk: HIGH on our normalization+transfer claim: this is an automatic interface-representation rewriter that explicitly GENERALIZES to UNSEEN tools, which collides with our 'normal form that transfers to held-out tools'. BUT (a) rewriting tool descriptions is explicitly inside our novelty boundary, (b) it optimizes descriptions for performance rather than mapping diverse representations to a canonical normal form, (c) no formal state-transition equivalence, (d) no metamorphic paired eval, (e) no model-ranking-stability claim, and (f) transfer is to unseen TOOLS, not to unseen TRANSFORMS/perturbation families. Our transfer-to-unseen-transforms + label-independence is the surviving delta.

title: Natural Language Tools: A Natural Language Approach to Tool Calling in Large Language Agents
authors: Reid T. Johnson; Michelle D. Pain; Jordan D. West
problem: Programmatic JSON tool-calling formats impose syntax constraints that degrade tool-selection accuracy.
unit_of_analysis: 10 models x 6400 trials across customer-service and mental-health domains.
intervention: Replace JSON function-call interface with a natural-language tool-calling representation, decoupling tool selection from response generation.
method: Comparative evaluation of JSON vs NL tool-calling representation; measures accuracy, variance, and robustness to prompt perturbation.
data: Customer service and mental-health task suites.
main_claim: NL representation lifts tool-calling accuracy by 18.4 pts and cuts output variance 70%; open-weight models gain most and SURPASS flagship closed-weight models.
relation_to_ours: HIGH on our ranking claim: changing the interface REPRESENTATION (JSON->NL) reorders models (open-weight overtakes closed-weight), evidence for our claim that representation changes model RANKING. BUT it proposes one alternative representation as a method rather than running a controlled metamorphic sweep over many behavior-preserving transforms with the state transition held identical; no formal state-transition equivalence, no paired metamorphic design, and no transform-label-free normalization layer with unseen-transform transfer. We generalize ranking-reversal from a single JSON-vs-NL swap to a metamorphic family and add normalization.

title: Model Context Protocol (MCP) Tool Descriptions Are Smelly! Towards Improving AI Agent Efficiency with Augmented MCP Tool Descriptions
authors: Mohammed Mehedi Hasan; Hao Li; Gopi Krishnan Rajbahadur; Bram Adams; Ahmed E. Hassan
unit_of_analysis: Natural-language MCP tool descriptions have quality defects ('smells') that hurt agent performance/efficiency.
method: Define a description-quality rubric/smells, scan tools, then augment descriptions and measure agent impact.
data: Six-component rubric + FM-based smell scanner; empirical before/after augmentation measurement.
relation_to_ours: 97.1% of descriptions contain >=1 smell; augmentation improves task success ~5.85 pts median but increases steps 67.46% and regresses 16.67% of cases.
collision_risk: MEDIUM: shows description-representation quality affects agent success and that rewriting/augmenting descriptions changes outcomes -- but augmenting/rewriting descriptions is squarely inside our novelty boundary. No state-transition equivalence definition, no metamorphic paired design, no ranking-reversal study, no canonical normal form transferring to unseen transforms. Useful as evidence that representation matters; not a competitor to our core method.

title: On the Robustness of Agentic Function Calling
problem: Function-calling robustness to non-malicious input perturbation is underexplored relative to raw accuracy.
unit_of_analysis: Function-calling models on an expanded BFCL subset.
intervention: Perturb (1) user queries (naturalistic variations) and (2) the toolkit (expand with semantically related tools).
method: Robustness benchmark measuring stability of function calling under the two perturbation families.
data: Expanded subset of the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (BFCL).
main_claim: Best models have critical robustness weaknesses to query variation and toolkit expansion, exposing gaps in current eval methodology.
relation_to_ours: MEDIUM: same high-level message ('perturbation changes function-calling outcomes') but perturbs the QUERY and the TOOLKIT membership, not the interface representation of a fixed tool with invariant state transition. No metamorphic PAIRED design over equivalent interfaces, no ranking-reversal analysis, no normalization layer. Reinforces motivation; does not pre-empt our core.

title: ToolSandbox: A Stateful Conversational Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities
authors: Jiarui Lu; Thomas Holleis; Yizhe Zhang; Bernhard Aumayer; Feng Nan; et al. (Apple)
problem: Prior tool-use evaluation is stateless, single-turn, or off-policy, missing stateful/interactive realism.
unit_of_analysis: LLM tool-use trajectories with stateful tools and inter-tool state dependencies.
intervention: Provide a stateful, on-policy, interactive benchmark with implicit state dependencies and milestone/minefield scoring.
method: Python-native execution context as world state; user simulator; dynamic milestone evaluation; includes a 'Canonicalization' task category.
main_claim: Open vs proprietary gap is large; State Dependency, Canonicalization, and Insufficient-Information tasks are hard for SOTA LLMs.
relation_to_ours: MEDIUM: provides the stateful-evaluation infrastructure our state-transition equivalence builds on, and even has a 'Canonicalization' notion -- but it canonicalizes user-provided arguments within tasks, not tool INTERFACE REPRESENTATIONS. No metamorphic paired interface manipulation, no equivalence-by-state-transition definition for interfaces, no ranking-reversal, no normalization-transfer claim. A foundation/baseline environment, not a collision on the contribution.

title: tau-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains
authors: Shunyu Yao; Noah Shinn; Pedram Razavi; Karthik Narasimhan
problem: Benchmarks do not test agents on user interaction or domain-policy adherence needed for deployment.
unit_of_analysis: Multi-turn agent-user conversations with domain API tools (retail
method: Evaluate end-to-end task completion by comparing final DATABASE STATE to an annotated goal state; introduce pass^k for reliability.
data: Simulated-user multi-turn rollouts; database-state-equality oracle; pass^k consistency metric.
relation_to_ours: SOTA function-calling agents solve <50% and are inconsistent (pass^8 <25% retail); state-comparison + pass^k expose reliability gaps.
collision_risk: MEDIUM: our equivalence-by-state-transition oracle is conceptually adjacent to tau-bench's final-database-state comparison, and pass^k is a reliability metric we may reuse. BUT tau-bench compares end states to score CORRECTNESS; it does not manipulate interface representation, has no metamorphic paired contrast between behavior-equivalent interfaces, no ranking-reversal study, and no normalization layer. Likely a building-block/baseline rather than a novelty collision.

title: Multi-Agent LLM-based Metamorphic Testing for REST APIs
authors: Shehroz Khan; Abdullah Mughees; Gaadha Sudheerbabu; Tanwir Ahmad; Dragos Truscan
problem: The test-oracle problem for REST APIs: hard to judge correctness when expected outputs are unknown.
unit_of_analysis: REST APIs documented with OpenAPI specs.
intervention: Use an LLM multi-agent workflow to discover and execute metamorphic test scenarios for APIs.
method: ARMeta: M/T/F-style multi-agent pipeline producing Given-When-Then metamorphic tests.
data: Two public web apps exposing REST interfaces.
main_claim: LLM-driven metamorphic test generation complements scenario-based API testing and finds additional behaviors.
relation_to_ours: MEDIUM: shares the 'metamorphic testing of tools/APIs' framing but the OBJECT under test is the API implementation (software correctness), whereas we test LLM-AGENT sensitivity to behavior-preserving INTERFACE representations. LLMs here are the test GENERATOR, not the system under test. No state-transition-equivalence-defined interface classes, no ranking analysis, no normalization layer. Cite to position our metamorphic framing; low risk of pre-empting the contribution.

title: AgentAtlas: Beyond Outcome Leaderboards for LLM Agents
problem: Outcome-only agent leaderboards hide control quality and are sensitive to measurement choices.
unit_of_analysis: Agent trajectories/control decisions; 1342 synthetic items x 8 models; audit of 15 benchmarks.
intervention: Introduce a control-decision taxonomy and trajectory-failure vocabulary; study how methodology choices affect rankings.
method: Six-state taxonomy (Act/Ask/Refuse/Stop/Confirm/Recover); compares taxonomy-aware vs taxonomy-blind prompt formats and evaluation axes.
data: Synthetic 1342-item set; survey of 15 agent benchmarks.
main_claim: Prompt framing (label menu present/absent) and axis choice can substantially change apparent model rankings; outcome-only leaderboards are fragile.
relation_to_ours: MEDIUM: directly supports our ranking-instability thesis (methodology choices reorder models) and even varies a PROMPT FORMAT, but the manipulated variable is the EVALUATION/labeling protocol and behavioral axis, not the tool INTERFACE representation with a fixed state transition. No metamorphic interface pairing, no state-transition equivalence, no normalization layer. Strong supporting citation; not a core collision.

title: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards
authors: Norah Alzahrani; Hisham Abdullah Alyahya; Yazeed Alnumay; Sultan Alrashed; et al.
problem: LLM leaderboard rankings are not robust to minor, benign benchmark/format changes.
unit_of_analysis: LLMs ranked on multiple-choice QA benchmarks (MMLU et al.).
intervention: Apply benign perturbations (choice order, answer-selection method, etc.) and observe ranking movement.
method: Systematic perturbation study across three categories; proposes a hybrid scoring method.
data: MMLU and other multiple-choice QA benchmarks.
main_claim: Minor format perturbations move models up to 8 leaderboard positions; single-format leaderboards are unreliable.
relation_to_ours: MEDIUM: the canonical prior result that FORMAT changes RANKING, which is one of our four novel cores -- a reviewer will cite it for 'ranking reversal is known'. BUT it operates on multiple-choice QA prompt format, NOT tool/agent interfaces, has no state-transition equivalence, no metamorphic tool pairing, and no normalization remedy. We extend ranking instability to behavior-preserving TOOL-INTERFACE transforms and add a normalization fix; this is our defensible delta over generic format-sensitivity.
